IMPACT.justification: The paper targets an important and underexplored problem—automatically learning cognitive (gaze-derived) features with CNNs for sentiment and sarcasm detection—and demonstrates that combining learned text and gaze features can outperform text-only and handcrafted-feature baselines (up to 11.27% improvement on sarcasm). However, gains are mixed and sometimes small or statistically insignificant (e.g., a 1.36% improvement for sarcasm in one setting), and evaluations rely on relatively small eye-tracking datasets with few readers per snippet, limiting immediate generalizability. Overall, the work offers a useful proof-of-concept that opens new research directions in integrating cognitive signals with deep models, but its impact is likely moderate until validated on larger, more diverse data and with more consistent performance.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well organized (introduction, method, experiments, conclusion) and states the main contributions early, describing a two-component CNN and the gaze+text fusion for sentiment and sarcasm. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate; hyperparameters are reported and datasets/baselines are clearly enumerated. One minor presentation gap is that the summary does not indicate whether figures, tables, or notation are consistently used or particularly effective, suggesting small room for improvement in illustrative material or notation clarity.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies a convolutional neural network to automatically learn cognitive (gaze-based) features and jointly models text and gaze for sentiment and sarcasm, which is a non-trivial extension over prior work that relied on handcrafted gaze/text features (Mishra et al., 2016). It clearly differentiates its contribution by comparing to those handcrafted-feature systems and to text-only CNN variants. However, the core idea is an application of existing CNN techniques to a previously explored modality (gaze) rather than a fundamentally new method, and reported gains are sometimes small and statistically insignificant, placing this work at a moderate (incremental-to-notable) level of novelty.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses sentiment and sarcasm detection using gaze-derived cognitive features combined with CNNs, which squarely falls within the remit of top-tier NLP conferences that welcome multimodal and cognitively informed language work. The methodology and evaluation match venue expectations (CNN models, comparisons to prior handcrafted-feature baselines such as Mishra et al. and Joshi et al., and use of published eye-tracking datasets with reported hyperparameters and accuracy metrics). My only reservation is potential interest from a core ML audience—because the contribution is an application of CNNs to a niche modality on relatively small datasets, it may be more of a strong fit for NLP than for a general ML venue, hence a minor scope concern.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates the model on two existing gaze-annotated datasets and compares against relevant baselines (handcrafted gaze/text systems and several text-only CNN variants), showing up to 11.27% gains while also noting a small/insignificant 1.36% improvement for sarcasm. Ablation-style comparisons (text-only vs. text+gaze CNNs) are present, but there are no thorough sensitivity analyses, limited discussion of statistical significance beyond one case, and hyperparameter tuning was not fully explored. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and some limitations (insignificant gains, tuning left to future work) are discussed, and hyperparameters are reported though code is not mentioned, so reproducibility may be incomplete.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate, recent baselines (Mishra et al. 2016 variants for sentiment and sarcasm) and several text-only CNN variants, using the same datasets and accuracy as the evaluation metric. It explicitly acknowledges when adding gaze yields only a small (1.36%) and statistically insignificant improvement, and reports cases of larger gains (up to 11.27%), with discussion of learned-filter differences. Coverage of related work is described as adequate, though the summary indicates minor omissions of other potential modern non-gaze baselines, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims are stated and are supported by experiments on two eye-tracking datasets with comparisons to sensible baselines (handcrafted gaze/text systems and text-only CNN variants), and it reports sizable gains in some settings (up to 11.27%) while acknowledging small/insignificant gains for sarcasm. However, the summary lacks details on train/test protocols, comprehensive statistical testing, and concrete evidence that learned CNN filters indeed capture the cited gaze phenomena; hyperparameters were chosen ad hoc. These omissions leave some uncertainty about robustness and generality of the claims, so the work is promising but has notable methodological gaps.

main_claims: Introduces a CNN-based framework that automatically learns cognitive features from eye-movement (gaze) data and textual input for sentiment polarity and sarcasm detection.

Combining automatically learned text and gaze features yields better classification performance than (i) CNN systems using text alone and (ii) existing systems that rely on handcrafted gaze and textual features.
The CNN can learn filters that capture gaze-related attributes (e.g., long-distance saccades, fixations, regressions, skips) analogous to learned image filters.

On the evaluated data, CNN model variants outperform traditional systems by up to 11.27% (for sarcasm detection), though the incremental gain from adding gaze to text CNNs can be small (1.36%) and statistically insignificant in some cases.
_summary.methodology: A two-component Convolutional Neural Network jointly processes text and gaze (eye-movement) inputs to learn feature representations automatically; learned features from both components are combined for classification of sentiment polarity and sarcasm.
datasets: Dataset 1 (Mishra et al., 2016a): 994 snippets (383 positive, 611 negative), 350 sarcastic; eye-movement data from 7 readers per snippet.
Dataset 2 (Joshi et al., 2014): 843 snippets (443 positive, 400 negative) of movie reviews and normalized tweets; eye-movement data from 5 readers per snippet.
baselines: Mishra et al. (2016c) systems using handcrafted text and gaze features (for sentiment).
Mishra et al. (2016b) sarcasm classifier (for sarcasm).
CNN variants using text-only (STATICTEXT, NONSTATICTEXT, MULTICHANNELTEXT).
limitations: Adding the gaze component to the CNN for sarcasm detection yielded only a 1.36% improvement which was statistically insignificant for that dataset.
Model hyper-parameters were fixed by trial and error; the authors state tuning might improve performance and is left as future work.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - sentiment analysis and sarcasm detection using cognitive (gaze) features and CNNs
